text: The surgeon begins mobilizing the terminal ileum by dissecting the peritoneum, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon performs a typical laparoscopic cholecystectomy using a medial approach, maneuvering instruments to access and remove the gallbladder.
The surgeon visualizes the surgical trunk on the superior mesenteric vein, highlighting its anatomical position and relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the iliocolic vein and the right colic vein, completing the vascular dissection in the laparoscopic view.
The surgeon performs a functional end-to-end anastomosis through an upper median small incision, connecting the resected bowel segments.
The surgeon initiates the cholecystectomy procedure, beginning the dissection of the gallbladder from its attachments.
The surgeon applies two clips to the cystic duct and then divides it, completing this step of the cholecystectomy.
Using the sonazoid view on the left side, two liver metastases are visualized in segment five.
The surgeon uses intraoperative sonographic examination to confirm the precise location of the liver tumors and their spatial relationship to major blood vessels.
The surgical team prepares to perform a pure laparoscopic subsegmentectomy on segment V of the liver, focusing on the area previously identified with river metastases.
Following mobilization of the right side of the liver, the surgeon begins the liver parenchymal dissection, carefully separating the liver tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates liver mobilization, which is crucial for minimizing blood loss during parenchymal transection by allowing the liver to be suspended.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic harmonic scalpel to open the country line, aiming to locate the right anterior Glissonian pedicle.
The main branch of the glistoneal pedicle is visualized entering segment V of the liver.
The surgeon continues the parenchymal dissection using a KUSA device, revealing additional branches of the glistoneal vessels into segment five of the liver.
The surgeon clips and divides both structures using the Enseal device, ensuring secure hemostasis during the liver resection.
The surgeon continues parenchymal dissection along the demarcation line using a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating liver tissue.
The surgeon completes the anatomical resection of segment five, ensuring both metastases are included within the resected specimen with a safe surgical margin.
The surgeon places the resected segment five of the liver into a sterile bag and retrieves it through the median incision, which was made for the anastomosis during the colectomy.
The surgeon measures the upper median incision, which is five centimeters in length.
